title: Supply Chain Multi-Tier Visibility and Geospatial Supplier Risk
dataSources: **ERP purchase orders.** SAP MM (`EKKO` header / `EKPO` line) or Oracle SCM (`PO_HEADERS_ALL` / line tables) via Splunk DB Connect 3.x (read-only JDBC, batched Rising Column or timestamp checkpoint). Normalize to `index=scm`, `sourcetype=erp:purchase_order` with line-level granularity. **Supplier risk enrichment.** External risk scoring feeds—Dun & Bradstreet supplier scores, Resilinc, Everstream, or similar—ingested via HTTP Event Collector (push) or scripted inputs (pull) into `sourcetype=supplier:scorecard`, aligned to `supplier_id` and `supplier_country`. **Key fields** (required for the composite model): `supplier_id`, `supplier_country`, `tier` (1 = direct / 2–3 = sub-tier), `on_time_pct`, `quality_accept_pct`, `geo_risk_index`, `part_number`, `order_value`, `lead_time_days`. **Volume / scale:** typical mid-market manufacturing sees roughly 10K–100K PO lines per month; size DB Connect batch windows and summary indexes so peer `stats` remain sub-minute at search time.
app: DB Connect 3.x ([Splunkbase 2686](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2686)) for ERP/MES data extraction. For real-time process data: Splunk Edge Hub with OPC-UA or Modbus TCP sources, or custom scripted inputs polling historian APIs. Lookup files for golden batch profiles managed via KV store or CSV.
spl:
index=scm sourcetype IN ("erp:purchase_order","supplier:scorecard")
| stats avg(on_time_pct) as delivery_score, avg(quality_accept_pct) as quality_score, latest(geo_risk_index) as geo_risk, dc(part_number) as parts_supplied, sum(order_value) as total_spend by supplier_id, supplier_country, tier
| eval composite_risk=round((100-delivery_score)*0.3 + (100-quality_score)*0.3 + geo_risk*0.4, 1)
| where composite_risk > 60 OR (tier=1 AND parts_supplied > 50 AND delivery_score < 90)
| sort -composite_risk
description: This use case rolls up on-time delivery rate, quality acceptance rate, and the latest geographic risk index for each supplier across supply-chain tiers and countries, then applies a weighted composite risk score and flags tier-one suppliers with many distinct parts and weak delivery performance.
value: Procurement and plant leadership can see concentration and fragility before a shortage stops a line, so they can qualify alternates, shift volume, or stage buffer stock while spend and customer commitments are still manageable instead of reacting after missed shipments.
implementation: Land normalized purchase-order and scorecard rows on `index=scm` with consistent `supplier_id` and `tier` (1 = direct strategic). Refresh supplier risk scores on a schedule that matches your third-party feed. Tune composite weights and the `composite_risk` and tier-one gates with sourcing; add a lookup for commodity family if one supplier masks many part numbers. **Domain context:** tier labels must match your bill-of-material mapping. **Splunk:** `latest()` on `geo_risk_index` assumes one risk row per bucket—collapse to `max()` if multiple events land in the window.
visualization: Dashboard layout — **Top row:** Panel 1 *Supplier geospatial heatmap* (choropleth or filled map by `supplier_country`, color by median `composite_risk`). **Second row:** Panel 2 *Top-N supplier risk ranking* (horizontal bar chart, `supplier_id` by `composite_risk`, stacked overlay optional for `tier`). **Third row, left:** Panel 3 *Concentration & spend detail table* (`supplier_id`, `tier`, `parts_supplied`, `delivery_score`, `quality_score`, `geo_risk`, `total_spend`, drilldown to PO lines). **Third row, right:** Panel 4 *Executive single value* (`dc(supplier_id)` above threshold vs prior period, with trend sparkline).

references.title: APICS — Supply Chain Operations Reference (SCOR) Model
Gartner — Supply Chain Risk Management Framework
FDA — Process Validation: General Principles and Practices (Guidance)
Splunk Lantern — Identifying and visualizing supply chain issues

knownFalsePositives: **Seasonal demand shifts.** When forecasts swing between peak build and offseason, trailing `on_time_pct` and `quality_accept_pct` averages can penalize suppliers who intentionally throttled capacity or prioritized other customers—composite risk spikes even though geopolitical exposure is unchanged. Mitigation: segment scores by fiscal quarter or seasonality index from S&OP; use exponentially weighted averages; freeze alerts during known demand transitions until two full reorder cycles complete. **Supplier M&A / entity renaming.** Dun & Bradstreet and ERP `supplier_id` keys diverge after acquisitions, divestitures, or DBA changes so risk scores attach to the wrong legal entity or duplicate rows inflate `parts_supplied`. Mitigation: maintain a `supplier_alias` lookup validated by procurement; nightly reconciliation job between MDM golden record and Splunk lookups; throttle alerts until alias coverage exceeds 98 percent for flagged suppliers. **Recipe / engineering change turbulence.** Concurrent ECO waves can crater `quality_accept_pct` temporarily while inspections catch up—geo and delivery metrics remain stable yet composite risk breaches. Mitigation: join ECO identifiers as an exclusion dimension; annotate alert context with engineering hold tags from PLM extracts; widen quality weight tolerance when ECO backlog KPI from quality exceeds threshold. **Planned maintenance and logistics mode changes.** Port rotations, mandated maintenance shutdowns, or modal shifts (ocean→air freight) distort on-time denominators versus the prior six-month baseline. Mitigation: ingest logistics calendar lookups; annotate PO lines with `transport_mode`; shift peer comparison windows to exclude maintenance blackouts validated by FMIS. **Raw material lot and specification drift.** Incoming material hardness or chemistry shifts can cascade to quality rejects without implying supplier financial or geo instability—risk models that over-weight acceptance rates ignore upstream commodity volatility. Mitigation: blend acceptance metrics with certificates of analysis fields; carve out commodities with volatility flags from APCA data; escalate only when deviations correlate across multiple unrelated part families from the same supplier.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud 9.2+ with DB Connect 3.x installed on a heavy forwarder or search head (per your deployment standard), plus network reachability to the ERP read replica hosting SAP MM (`EKKO`, `EKPO`) or Oracle SCM (`PO_HEADERS_ALL`, `PO_LINES_ALL`) with a least-privilege database user limited to SELECT on purchase-order views.
• HTTP Event Collector (HEC) or Heavy Forwarder with scripted input capability for supplier risk APIs (Dun & Bradstreet, Resilinc, Everstream, or internal composite scores), including API keys stored in the credential store—not in props.
• A governed index (`scm`, or equivalent) with retention aligned to S&OP cycles (typically 13–18 months of PO lines for seasonality), and optional summary index `scm_supplier_daily` for executive rollups.
• Master data agreement on `supplier_id` join keys between ERP, MDM, and risk vendors; currency normalization rules for `order_value`; and numeric typing for `on_time_pct` / `quality_accept_pct` (0–100, not strings with “%”).
• Reference data for `tier` mapping (1/2/3) traceable to bill-of-material or procurement policy; UAT sign-off dataset of at least ten suppliers with known geo and performance outcomes.
• RBAC: procurement analytics role can search `index=scm`; supplier risk team can view scorecard sourcetype; alert actions limited to non-PII channels.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Stand up two parallel ingestion paths. **Path A — ERP PO lines via DB Connect:** create a connection to the read replica; define a rising-column input (for example `LAST_UPDATE_DATE` or `ERNAM` + `EBELN` composite) that pulls incremental PO line changes every 15–30 minutes for near-real-time risk, or hourly if volume is high. Project SQL should emit one row per PO line with `supplier_id`, `supplier_country`, `tier`, `part_number`, `order_value`, `lead_time_days`, receipt-based `on_time_pct` if computed in ERP, or raw dates so Splunk can derive performance. Use `props.conf` on the heavy forwarder to assign `sourcetype=erp:purchase_order`, `LINE_BREAKER` for NDJSON or single-line CSV, `TIME_FORMAT` / `TIME_PREFIX` for the ERP timestamp, and `TRUNCATE` high enough for wide rows. In `transforms.conf`, optional `SEDCMD` strips thousands separators; `FIELDALIAS` maps Oracle/SAP column names to canonical Splunk fields. Land data on `index=scm` with source naming `dbconnect:erp_po`. **Path B — Supplier risk scores:** prefer HEC JSON posts from the vendor’s push integration; otherwise schedule a Python or PowerShell scripted input with exponential backoff, writing one event per supplier-country or supplier-id scorecard refresh. Map to `sourcetype=supplier:scorecard` with `geo_risk_index` as a 0–100 scale consistent with procurement’s interpretation. If multiple scores arrive per day, retain `_raw` ingestion order and rely on `latest()` only after verifying single latest per key. **Lookup hygiene:** publish `supplier_alias.csv` linking legacy IDs to golden `supplier_id`. Schedule `rest` or `_internal` monitors for DB Connect job failures and HEC token rate limits.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Once sample data is searchable, normalize fields at search time if DB column names drift: `| eval supplier_id=coalesce(supplier_id,VENDOR_ID)` etc. Paste the correlation search:

```spl
index=scm sourcetype IN ("erp:purchase_order","supplier:scorecard")
| stats avg(on_time_pct) as delivery_score, avg(quality_accept_pct) as quality_score, latest(geo_risk_index) as geo_risk, dc(part_number) as parts_supplied, sum(order_value) as total_spend by supplier_id, supplier_country, tier
| eval composite_risk=round((100-delivery_score)*0.3 + (100-quality_score)*0.3 + geo_risk*0.4, 1)
| where composite_risk > 60 OR (tier=1 AND parts_supplied > 50 AND delivery_score < 90)
| sort -composite_risk
```

Understanding this SPL
The search unions purchase-order operational facts with supplier risk overlays in one index, relying on sourcetype discrimination inside `stats` field aggregations—that assumes risk attributes are replicated onto PO events or emitted as separate rows that share dimensional keys (`supplier_id`, `supplier_country`, `tier`). In deployments where scorecards are standalone, refactor with `eventstats` or a `lookup` after `stats` by supplier. `avg(on_time_pct)` and `avg(quality_accept_pct)` implement smoothers over heterogeneous parts; outliers can be tempered with `trim` using `perc95`. `latest(geo_risk_index)` assumes time-ordered ingestion; swap to `max()` if geopolitical feeds are monotonic worsening scales. `dc(part_number)` measures portfolio breadth—a concentration proxy complementary to monetary `sum(order_value)`. The `eval` expresses a convex combination: 40% geopolitical emphasis reflects executive appetite for disruptive events, while 60% emphasizes execution quality—weights should be parameterized via macros (`scm_weight_geo`, etc.) so sourcing can simulate scenarios. Threshold logic encodes governance: numeric composite breaches OR strategic tier-one fragility signals (many parts, weak delivery). This is heuristic, not Benford—which applies to fraudulent disbursement—not supply analytics; nonetheless, add a supplemental `audit` correlation using first-digit anomalies on invoice amounts (`eval leading=substr(tostring(round(order_value,2)),1,1)`) weekly for finance alignment.

Schedule as Alert
Cron **5 7 * * *** (07:05 local) scanning **Last 90 days**, trigger **Number of Results > 0**, alert **once per day** digest with throttle key `supplier_id` (suppress repeat for 72h unless `composite_risk` rises 10 points). For war-room paging, duplicate alert with cron ***/30 * * * *** on **Last 24h** gated to `tier=1` only.

Step 3 — Validate
Always reconcile Splunk aggregates against the authoritative **ERP purchasing UI or reporting workbench** (for example SAP GUI transactions ME2N/ME2L, Fiori purchasing analytics tiles, Oracle SCM Purchasing HTML pages) plus the supplier risk vendor **portal UI** snapshots for the cited effective dates before engaging suppliers commercially.
**(a)**(a)** Reconcile `total_spend` for the top ten suppliers versus SAP BW / Oracle Purchasing spend cube for identical calendar window and FX table. **(b)** Confirm `tier` classifications against six blind-sampled supplier master rows. **(c)** Spot-check five PO lines—recompute on-time receipts vs promised date in ERP and compare to averaged field. **(d)** Align `geo_risk_index` to vendor dashboard screenshots at score effective date ±1 day. **(e)** Inspect `parts_supplied` uniqueness—ensure phantom EBELN cancellations are filtered upstream. **(f)** Chaos test DB Connect failover: pause job 2h and verify alerting on `_internal`/DB Connect error patterns so silent gaps cannot masquerade as improved risk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard:** use the four panels described under `visualization`—map heat, ranked bar, detail table with drilldown, KPI single-value. **Alerting:** route daily digest email to Chief Procurement Officer alias; webhook to Teams channel `#supply-risk`; Critical subset to PagerDuty service `procurement-geopolitical`. **Runbook:** (1) confirm DB Connect watermark advanced, (2) validate risk vendor feed latency, (3) compare anomaly to Acts of God calendar, (4) open sourcing task in CLM requesting dual qualification, (5) if tier-one chokepoint, escalate to Demand-Inventory war room with alternate routing plan, (6) document rationale in Governance repository for auditors.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
**Symmetric null delivery scores:** ERP stopped sending receipts—inspect DB Connect logs and JDBC errors. **`latest(geo_risk)` flatlines:** stale risk API credential; rotate API key. **Sudden spike in `parts_supplied`:** BOM explosion from phantom alternates MDM duplication—reload alias lookup. **`composite_risk` all zeros:** percentages ingested as strings; fix `FIELDALIAS` + `convert auto(*)`. **Score inflation after ERP go-live:** historical window blends legacy and new ERP IDs—narrow earliest time bound. **Join fan-out duplicates spend:** multi-line risk events per PO line—pre-dedup with `| dedup supplier_id _time` upstream. **Search slow beyond 90d:** migrate to summary index macro `summary_scm_supplier`.

Supplementary depth — enterprise supply chain analytics hardening
This section extends the base five-step flow with implementation detail typical of global discrete manufacturing programs that must defend combined ratio–like capital exposure in materials spend while satisfying SOX-style IT general controls over analytics changes. Treat Splunk content (macros, saved searches, lookups) as configuration items in the same change board as ERP transports so threshold drift is auditable. When sourcing DB Connect SQL for SAP, prefer packaged extractors that select `EBELN`, `EBELP`, `LIFNR`, `MATNR`, `MENGE`, `NETWR`, goods-movement-reconciled delivery timestamps, and incoterm fields if you later enrich logistics risk. For Oracle, align to `PO_HEADERS_ALL`, `PO_LINES_ALL`, and vendor sites with `VENDOR_ID` joins; cast numeric columns explicitly in SQL to avoid implicit conversion errors that silently truncate decimals in JDBC. For supplier risk APIs, store raw JSON in a development index for replay debugging but production should project only whitelisted fields to reduce PII and license leakage from vendor contracts. Implement idempotent HEC posts with a signature header so replays deduplicate on `supplier_id` + `score_effective_ts`.

Network controls often require allow-listing vendor IP ranges and rotating OAuth client secrets; document these in the same runbook as disaster recovery because geopolitical escalations spike both feed latency and page volume. Build a **macro layer**—for example `scm_weight_delivery`, `scm_weight_quality`, `scm_weight_geo`, `scm_tier1_parts_min`, `scm_composite_threshold`—and reference them from the saved search so business tuning does not require editing raw SPL. Pair the composite model with a secondary **concentration** alert that uses Herfindahl-style spend shares at commodity family level; financial risk may appear before supplier execution degrades. Add **Benford** or first-two-digit tests on `order_value` by `supplier_country` weekly; while not part of the primary SPL, finance teams value the control narrative in integrated audits. When harmonizing `geo_risk_index` scales between vendors, normalize min-max per feed or z-score within feed before blending; otherwise a rescoring event looks like a mass incident.

Operational validation should include **cross-footing** payment terms: sum `order_value` by month against AP accrual extracts to catch sign inversions on returns. For testing, seed synthetic suppliers in lower environments with controlled score spikes and confirm alert routing and throttle keys behave as expected. Training materials for analysts should cover how `latest(geo_risk_index)` differs from `max()` and when procurement should request replacement with `max()` if the vendor issues severity scores that are non-decreasing. Finally, align dashboard narrative to APICS SCOR metrics—Plan, Source, Make, Deliver, Return—so executives see how Splunk detection maps to processes they already govern. Archive monthly PDF snapshots of the dashboard into your GRC tool if external auditors expect evidence of continuous monitoring.

Secondary supplement — alerting, dashboards, evidence, and continuous improvement cadence
Design **two-tier alerting**: a daily consolidated email digest sourced from summary indexing of the nightly run, plus a streaming micro-alert for geopolitical escalation tags when incremental risk scores jump more than fifteen points versus prior weekday. Tie alert descriptions to SCM war-room RACI matrices so each recipient knows expected actions inside twenty-four hours. Use adaptive response placeholders for deep links back to Procurement CLM workflows and sanctioned alternate vendor lists maintained as lookups refreshed weekly. Embed **forecast error KPIs**: when demand forecasts upstream systematically mis-hit, supplier performance KPIs degrade—splunk correlation from IBP extracts helps analysts separate supplier execution misses from forecasting bias.

Evidence packaging for audits should include SPL export, screenshot of thresholds, lineage diagram from ERP replica through DB Connect to index, vendor contract exhibit permitting external risk ingestion, and a change log excerpt for macro tuning. Maintain **purple team** tabletop exercises blending sourcing, treasury, legal, and IT to simulate sanctions shock and supplier insolvency cascades validating alert latency under stress. Quarterly model reviews quantify alert precision/recall proxies using adjudicated outcomes annotated in KV store `supplier_alert_outcomes`.

Performance-wise, accelerate heavy periods by writing `summaryidx=scm_supplier_risk_daily` captures from the base stats pipeline; dashboards then query summaries with faster refresh. Leverage Splunk Observability workflows if JDBC fetch durations jitter—tracing DB latency sometimes precedes stale risk composites. Extend documentation with glossary entries for tiers, sourcing categories, commodity codes NTMS/UNSPSC mappings, harmonized export classification where applicable. Where Edge Hub overlaps for OT KPIs unrelated to SCM, segregate naming conventions preventing sourcetype collisions. Disaster recovery rehearsals must validate rising column resume points preventing duplicate PO lines—which otherwise corrupt averages and falsely demonize suppliers. Finally, socialize SCOR-aligned metrics decks monthly so CFO, CSCO, plant managers share vocabulary about detection purpose.

Tertiary supplement — reference architectures, KPI bridges, resilience testing, governance
Publish a reference topology diagram showing ERP read replicas, Splunk Heavy Forwarders with DB Connect, optional secrets vault integration, indexer clustering, search head pooling, and HEC ingestion paths separated by security zone labels (Corp, Hybrid OT boundary if logistics sensors ever enrich PO context). Narrate failover: if the primary indexer site is offline, how secondary search peers continue scheduled supplier risk jobs without duplicating rising column checkpoints—document idempotent SQL predicates and DB Connect job clustering awareness. Bridge analytics to executive KPIs by exporting weekly summarized risk counts into the corporate Snowflake semantic layer consumed by CFO office Tableau dashboards, making Splunk the detection engine—not the canonical financial ledger.

Run quarterly chaos experiments: revoke HEC tokens temporarily to verify stale-data monitors fire; throttle ERP extracts to emulate partial-day outages and observe composite score stability; inject synthetic geopolitical shocks in lower environments respecting vendor license constraints. Institutionalize RACI overlays with procurement legal for sanctions escalation paths when composite risk aligns with sanctioned entity screenings from separate compliance tooling—analytics must not adjudicate sanctions but must route signals expediently. Close with playbook for **model deprecation**: when replacing composite weights, retain prior macro revision numbers in Git tags for retrospective investigations tied to disputed supplier scorecards lingering in contractual disputes.

Quaternary supplement — SPL hygiene, indexer sizing, observability loops, stakeholder communications
Operational teams should encapsulate repetitive `eval` normalizations (`supplier_id`, `material_id`) inside **macros** `m_normalize_scm`, `m_normalize_inv` respecting least-privilege edits via `default.meta`. Document **Splunk ingest lag SLO**: if median `_indextime-_time` drifts beyond agreed minutes, degrade alert automation to investigative-only mode until ingestion recovers—embed this policy in Incident Response playbooks bridging ITOps and analytics owners. Estimate daily indexed volume from DB Connect PO line widths (bytes/row × rows/day) multiplied by archival retention to avoid surprise license overrun; revisit SmartStore ratios quarterly.

Maintain **Synthetic event canaries**: lightweight scheduled searches injecting tagged harmless events validating index routing end-to-end. Training communications should include glossary bridging business concepts (tier, BOM, lapse) versus Splunk field names minimizing confusion escalation noise. Celebrate wins publicly when analytics prevented outages or leakage—cultural reinforcement strengthens continuous funding. Periodic third-party Red Team review of alert logic ensures thresholds remain defensible under adversarial gaming (suppliers gaming on-time metrics with early premature GR postings, etc.). Finally, align documentation with corporate knowledge base standards (Confluence / SharePoint) including ownership matrix, review cadence, retirement criteria for deprecated macros—good lifecycle hygiene prevents stale guidance misdirecting new analysts during attrition transitions commonly experienced dynamic industries.

Cross-functional assurance and questionnaires
Hold monthly sourcing–treasury–analytics forums tying macro weight changes and exception backlogs to ERM heat maps. Invite internal audit annually to replicate historical alert runs against archived buckets for reproducibility demonstrations supporting ISO 28000 or third-party risk questionnaires. When consultants assess supply networks, sanitized Splunk PDFs showcase continuous monitoring maturity. Track API rate-limit telemetry and contractual DPA clauses for each risk-intelligence supplier to avoid unintentional redistribution of proprietary scores. Respect anti-corruption workflows by routing high-risk supplier hits into due diligence queues without circumventing localized privacy laws.

Platform operations and continuous improvement (cross-cutting)
Maintain version-controlled exports of saved search definitions or Splunk ACS app bundles so promotions from development to production stay diff-reviewed. Map service accounts to LDAP-backed Splunk roles that separate macro authors from analysts who run searches read-only on production. Track scheduler duration, skipped search counts, and search peer saturation on an internal observability dashboard; degradations frequently foreshadow muted alerts. Coordinate vulnerability management for DB Connect, Edge Hub forwarders, and HEC endpoints with cybersecurity patch cycles, followed by scripted regression reruns using frozen time windows and canned queries. Establish legal hold-aware retention discussions with records management before litigation freezes supplier or claims evidence; know whether to pause bucket rolling deletion. Run quarterly cross-training so backup owners can execute runbooks. Auto-open tickets with SPL excerpts, alert titles, and deep links. Review alert precision with labeled outcomes monthly. Partner with FinOps on license efficiency—summary indexing and proper source typing reduce cost per insight. Forward critical `scheduler` and `deployer` messages to centralized monitoring so the monitoring platform itself stays observable. These habits stabilize analytics programs irrespective of vertical specifics.
grandmaExplanation: We line up who sends us parts, how reliable they are, and whether their corner of the world looks shaky. When too many eggs sit in one basket, we spot it early so folks can reroute orders before shelves go bare.
